## [00:00 - 03:03]

Cash flow is king. In fact, cash is almost worthless. And again, if you have an asset that's going down in value, cash, in real estate that's going up in value. You want to take a depreciating asset and use it to purchase an appreciating asset. Cash is not king. Cash flow is king. Leverage will take that cash flow and actually multiply it. You're not going to buy low, steal stuff, great products, great locations, great real estate is not going to be stolen. Okay? In fact, my best deals have been property that I paid more money for, not less. So this old notion that you're going to buy low and sell high, that's the only way you're going to try to buy real estate. You're going to buy the junk left over in the marketplace. This is an old adage and it's incorrect. You want to buy great real estate at a fair price, not terrible product at a low price. And you want to build a buy great assets that cash flow. You need to focus on cash flow, not price. If you have positive cash flow and time, cash flow and time, plus the number of units that scale your operations, you will drive appreciation that will be massive in the future, but you have to have cash flow. Why? Because the cash flow gives you time during bad times. You don't want to sell your property when things are terrible. You want to sell it when things are great. If it happens that you have cash flow during bad times, you can't and won't lose money in real estate no matter where you start, what price you start at, when you start it and where you buy in. But where you buy in the type of product is vital. That piece is important time. There's a quote that says, don't wait to buy real estate, buy real estate and wait. And it's timely here. No pun. Buy real estate and wait. Don't wait and buy real estate. However, you'll need to have cash flow while this happens. If you don't have the cash flow, you will not be able to work your way through the ups and downs. If you have positive cash flow, you can indulge yourself with time. Sometimes even when values go down and they can, your cash flow will stay consistent. Sometimes go down a little bit, but it could even increase. Let's take an example. You buy a building that's worth $200,000 and you collect $2,000 in rent monthly. One year the value dips by even 20%. 20% or $200,000 is $40,000.

## [03:03 - 06:07]

Not potential rent, but actual received rent, effective gross rent. Look at the real financial statements and ignore things like gross possible income and potential income. Focus on what did we actually collect on the real numbers and the real money that was collected by the real tenants. ## [12:11 - 13:29]

That's my cash flow. I take that cash flow, that chunk, and I take it. It's going down in value. It's no good that money's going down in value. Almost everyone agrees with that. I take that and buy assets that are appreciating and that will provide me with more cash flow. I need the new cash flow. I took this chunk of cash from my work, my job, and I invested it in something that can generate passive cash flow to me to give me time so that the property can go up in value over five years, eight years, 10 years, or longer. Calculating cash flow is vital. While you'll need to work through the calculations outlined here and future modules, our rule of thumb is to look for properties that have expenses at or less than 1% per month. So our rule of thumb, our rule of thumb is this. I'm going to take a property that generates $1 million of income and I'm going to cut it in half right away. 50% is going to go to expenses. The real number is going to be more like 35 to 44% of the gross revenue will hit the expense line and leave me with the remainder to pay my debt. That number, NOI, less my debt, will be my cash flow.
